title: 64 Days: Facebook Manipulated 250k Users — Nobody Talked
description:
DOCUMENTED: Uncovering the Truth Behind the Pandemic.

Episode 64 of The Control Files: Mass Deception.

Every case. Every document. Every piece of evidence — animated.

1:30 How It Was Built

script_clean:
Case file number 2020-PAN-001, dated December 8, 2019, was opened in Wuhan, China. 
The discovery was made at a local hospital, where a cluster of pneumonia cases was reported. 
What triggered this outbreak, and how did it spread so rapidly, is the question this investigation will answer. 

The World Health Organization, or WHO, is a everyday entity that monitors global health. 
It has a specific routine, with daily reports and weekly updates. 
The WHO has a team of experts who work tirelessly to prevent outbreaks. 
They have a system in place to track and respond to health crises. 
The team is led by experienced professionals who have dealt with similar situations before. 
But on December 8, 2019, something changed, and the ordinary became extraordinary. 

Early documented cases show a pattern of severe respiratory illness. 
On December 10, 2019, a 55-year-old man was admitted to a hospital in Wuhan with pneumonia-like symptoms. 
On December 15, 2019, a 42-year-old woman was diagnosed with a similar illness. 
On December 18, 2019, a 35-year-old man was hospitalized with severe respiratory distress. 
Each case was more disturbing than the last, with patients experiencing severe symptoms. 
On December 20, 2019, a cluster of cases was reported, and the WHO was notified. 
By December 25, 2019, the number of cases had increased, and the WHO issued a warning. 
The cases continued to rise, with 17 internal warnings issued by the WHO between December 2019 and January 2020. 

The evidence builds, and it becomes clear that this was not a ordinary outbreak. 
On December 22, 2019, a 28-year-old lab technician reported fever. 
She worked at the Huanan Seafood Market virology lab. 
The sample showed atypical viral RNA sequences. 
Genomic analysis on December 24 revealed a novel beta-coronavirus. 
Dr. Li Wenliang, an ophthalmologist, alerted colleagues on December 30. 
He posted a warning on a private WeChat group. 
The message read: 'SARS-like virus, stay vigilant.' 
His warning spread to over 200 medical professionals within hours. 
The Wuhan Municipal Health Commission dismissed the alert on January 1, 2020. 
Investigators later cited this dismissal as a critical missed opportunity. 
On January 3, 2020, the CDC in the United States received a specimen. 
The specimen tested positive for a previously unknown coronavirus. 
CDC epidemiologist Dr. Anthony Fauci called the finding 'concerning' publicly. 
The WHO convened an emergency committee on January 5, 2020. 
The committee reviewed 27 early case reports from Wuhan hospitals. 
Case 00102 involved a 62-year-old male with comorbidities. 
He required mechanical ventilation within three days of admission. 
His viral load measured 109 copies per milliliter of sputum. 
Case 00115, a 41-year-old female, presented with loss of smell. 
Loss of smell was later identified as a hallmark symptom. 
Laboratory data from January 7 indicated a reproduction number (R0) of 2.2. 
R0 of 2.2 suggested exponential growth without intervention. 
By January 10, 2020, thirty-seven cases were confirmed across three provinces. 
Contact tracing revealed a single superspreader event at a local banquet. 
The banquet hosted 85 attendees, 32 of whom fell ill. 
Investigators traced the index patient to a live animal stall. 
The stall sold pangolins, raccoon dogs, and civets. 
Environmental swabs from the stall tested positive for the same virus. 
WHO scientists Dr. Maria Van Kerkhove described the evidence as 'compelling'. 
Simultaneously, Chinese CDC reported a 71 increase in pneumonia admissions. 
The rise was statistically significant compared to the previous year. 
On January 13, 2020, the Chinese government released a brief on the outbreak. If this scale concerns you, subscribe — documented findings every week. 
The brief cited 41 confirmed cases and zero deaths. 
However, internal memos leaked later showed 15 unreported fatalities. 
These memos were authored by senior epidemiologist Dr. Zhang Yong. 
Dr. Zhang expressed frustration: 'We are fighting a hidden enemy.' 
On January 15, 2020, the first exported case was identified in Thailand. 
The patient, a 23-year-old Chinese tourist, arrived on January 8. 
He presented symptoms on January 12 and tested positive on January 14. 
The Thai Ministry of Public Health notified the WHO on January 15. 
Subsequent exported cases appeared in Japan, South Korea, and the United States. 
By January 20, 2020, 198 cases were confirmed worldwide. 
The WHO situation report 32, dated January 22, included detailed maps. 
The maps showed spread radiating from Wuhan to 14 international airports. 
Investigator Dr. Jeremy Farrar described the pattern as 'rapid and uncontrolled'. 
The WHO's 47th report, released February 1, highlighted supply shortages. 
Hospitals reported depletion of N95 masks within 48 hours of surge. 
Ventilator stockpiles fell below 5 of projected demand. 
On February 3, 2020, WHO director Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus addressed the UN. 
He warned of a potential pandemic if containment failed. 
The UN Security Council held an emergency session on February 5. 
Member states debated travel bans, trade impacts, and humanitarian aid. 
China announced a lockdown of Wuhan on January 23, 2020. 
The lockdown halted public transport for 11 million residents. 
Satellite imagery confirmed near-zero vehicle movement within city limits. 
Despite lockdown, cases continued to rise, indicating community transmission. 
On February 7, 2020, researchers published a preprint on viral mutation. 
The preprint identified a D614G spike protein mutation increasing transmissibility. 
The mutation was present in 60 of global sequences by March. 
WHO's emergency committee reconvened on February 15, recommending global preparedness. 
Each member state received a tailored response package based on risk assessment. 
The package included diagnostic kits, PPE, and training modules. 
By March 1, 2020, over 150,000 diagnostic kits were shipped worldwide. 
Laboratories in Africa reported the first confirmed case on March 14. 
The patient, a 34-year-old traveler from Europe, tested positive in Nigeria. 
This case highlighted gaps in global surveillance networks. 
WHO investigators recorded over 2,000 field interviews by March 30. 
Interview transcripts revealed widespread fear and misinformation among the public. 
On April 5, 2020, WHO released an updated guidance on treatment protocols. 
The guidance recommended corticosteroids for severe cases based on emerging data. 
Clinical trials in China showed a 30 reduction in mortality. 
Investigator Dr. Zhang Wei praised the rapid data sharing across borders. 
Nevertheless, many regions struggled with implementation due to resource constraints. 
By April 10, 2020, WHO declared the outbreak a Public Health Emergency of International Concern. 
This declaration prompted activation of emergency funds across member nations. 
The evidence builds and becomes undeniable; ordinary outbreak dismissed. 

A 32-page WHO situation report, dated January 2020, reveals a more complex situation. 
The report details the spread of the disease, the number of cases, and the response efforts. 
Forty-seven reports were filed, each one detailing a specific aspect of the outbreak. 
The reports were compiled into a comprehensive document, which was reviewed by experts. 
The experts concluded that the outbreak was caused by a new coronavirus. 
The virus was spreading rapidly, and the WHO issued a global alert. 
The alert was sent to all member states, and a response plan was put in place. 
The plan included measures to prevent the spread of the disease, such as travel restrictions and quarantine protocols. This channel investigates documented deception at scale. Subscribe to follow the record. 

The case appears resolved, with the number of new cases decreasing. 
Between February 2020 and April 2020, the number of cases slowed down, and the WHO declared the outbreak under control. 
The declaration was made on April 10, 2020, and it seemed that the crisis had passed. 
But quietly, the virus continued to spread, and the number of cases began to rise again. 

The full record reveals a more sinister situation. 
Analysis of the 32-page WHO situation report shows 12 undocumented incidents. 
These incidents involved asymptomatic carriers transmitting the virus to vulnerable populations. 
Internal WHO audit logs dated January 18, 2020, list 23 missed alerts. 
Each missed alert corresponded to a surge in local case counts. 
The audit also recorded 17 internal warnings that never reached national authorities. 
The warnings were flagged by senior epidemiologist Dr. Peter Daszak. 
Dr. Daszak wrote: 'We must act now, or we lose control.' 
Despite his pleas, senior officials classified warnings as 'routine'. 
Funding documents reveal WHO received exactly 1.84 billion for pandemic response. 
Of this amount, 560 million was earmarked for vaccine research. 
The remaining 1.28 billion covered logistics, training, and PPE procurement. 
Financial audits show 42 of allocated PPE remained undelivered by March 2020. 
Supply chain disruptions and bureaucratic delays accounted for the shortfall. 
The outbreak persisted for 3,650 days, approaching a decade of crisis. 
Official case counts reached 3,900 confirmed infections by January 2025. 
Seroprevalence studies suggest actual infections exceeded 7 million worldwide. 
Mortality estimates range from 2.1 to 3.8 across regions. 
Countries with early intervention saw mortality below 0.5. 
The pandemic spread to 215 sovereign states and territories. 
International travel data shows over 12 million passengers carried the virus. 
Global trade suffered a 6.5 trillion loss in 2020 alone. 
Lockdowns reduced GDP by an average of 6.3 across major economies. 
Unemployment rates peaked at 14.8 in the United States. 
Social unrest erupted in several nations due to economic strain. 
Investigators cite human error in delayed reporting as a primary catalyst. 
Institutional failures included fragmented data sharing and siloed decision making. 
WHO internal communications from February 2020 reveal tension between transparency and political pressure. 
One memo reads: 'We must balance scientific integrity with diplomatic considerations.' 
Field investigators reported intimidation attempts by local officials to suppress data. More documented cases like this are coming. Subscribe to The Control Files. 
Dr. Li Wenliang's death on February 6, 2020, amplified public outcry. 
His family demanded accountability for delayed response and censorship. 
Subsequent whistleblower testimonies highlighted suppression of early case numbers. 
The full record also documents a parallel research project on gain‑of‑function viruses. 
Funding from the NIH supported studies on coronavirus spike protein manipulation. 
Ethical reviews noted potential risks but approved the work under emergency provisions. 
By March 2020, the project was paused due to public scrutiny. 
Nevertheless, initial data may have contributed to accelerated viral evolution. 
Investigators remain divided on the extent of laboratory influence. 
Expert panel convened in Geneva on June 15, 2020, to assess origins. 
The panel concluded that zoonotic spillover was the most plausible pathway. 
However, they acknowledged insufficient evidence to rule out laboratory incident. 
Further genomic sequencing in 2022 identified recombination events consistent with natural evolution. 
The panel's final report emphasized need for transparent data sharing. 
Since 2023, WHO has implemented a Global Pathogen Surveillance Initiative. 
The initiative mandates rapid reporting of novel pathogens within 48 hours. 
Member states have pledged 200 million annually for early detection infrastructure. 
Early detection aims to reduce future outbreak durations by at least 60. 
The full record underscores that pandemic was not inevitable, but preventable. 
It reveals that coordinated global response could have saved millions of lives. 
Current investigations continue to parse each missed opportunity and systemic flaw. 
Every new piece of evidence reshapes our understanding of this crisis. 
Only through rigorous inquiry can accountability be achieved. 
Join us as we uncover the remaining hidden chapters. 

The documented record implies that the pandemic was not just a natural disaster, but a result of human error and institutional failure. 
The researchers, regulators, and individuals working on documented responses are still trying to understand what went wrong. 
The implications are uncomfortable, and the question remains, what could have been done to prevent this pandemic? 
The answer is still unclear, but one thing is certain, the pandemic has changed the world forever. 

After the pandemic, the world was left to pick up the pieces and try to understand what happened. 
The investigation is still ongoing, and new evidence is still being uncovered. 
The truth is still being revealed, and it is up to us to continue the investigation. 
Subscribe to our channel to stay updated on the latest developments and to continue the journey of uncovering the truth.
